coredata.dc:title: DNA-based bioassay of legionella pneumonia pathogen using gold nanostructure: A new platform for diagnosis of legionellosis
coredata.dc:creator: Mobed, Ahmad
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Babaie, Parinaz
Agazadeh, Mohammad
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Rezaee, Mohammad Ahangarzadeh
coredata.dc:description: The specific diagnosis of hard-growing bacteria is one of the most important concerns of medical bacteriology. Legionella pneumophila is one of the most important bacteria in hard growth. In spite remarkable trends in bacteriology, now day, culture is the gold standard for detection of L. pneumophila. This work is an attempt to quantification of L. pneumophila bacteria using a bioassay. The fabrication of a new electrochemical DNA-based bioassay using gold nano architecture combined with as a transducer substrate combined with toluidine blue (TB) as a redox marker was performed successful. Also, the mixture of beta‑cyclodextrin and dopamine as Poly (dopamine‑β‑Cyclodextrin) was used to proper a biointerface for stabilization of gold nanoparticles optimum immobilize of pDNA sequence (5–SH-TCGA TAC TCT CCC CGC CCC TT T TGTATCGACG–3). So, a specific thiolated pDNA was immobilized on the transducer substrate and DNA hybridization was followed by C-DNA sequence (5–ACA AAA GGG GCG GGG AGA GTA–3) using square wave voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry. At the optimum conditions, linear range was 1 μM to 1 ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zepto-molar. L. pneumophila were sensitively distinguished by the planned DNA sensor. Finally, the engineered DNA based bioassay could be used for identifying the L. pneumophila samples from patients or environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hard-growing pathogen
Bioassay
Oligonucleotide sequences
Legionella pneumonia
Gold nanoparticle

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electroactive substrate (poly (dopamine-beta cyclodextrin) modified gold nano-rod supported by chitosan) (CS)‑Au/P(DA‑β‑CD)
Characterization of interfaces and engineered biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was supported by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences . This work is part of PhD thesis of A. Mobed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique of Au electrode in the solution containing 0.02 g of dopamine + 0.01 g of β-CD. Sweep rate was 50 mV/s. Number of cycle is 20. (B) Chronoamperograms of Au electrode pre-modified by Poly (DA-β-CD in the solution of 0.005 M HAuCl4 + 0005 g of chitosan. E = 1.5 V. t = 3 min.
CVs of the Au electrode, and the DNA biosensor before and after hybridization with c-DNA in a solution of 20 μM TB dissolved in 0.01 M Tris-HCl. The potential sweep rate was 100 mV/s.

A) SWVs recorded using the DNA biosensor after binding with 20 μM of TB in Tris-HCl after different binding times of 2, 5, 7, 10 and 15 min. B) Histogram of peak currents versus incubation time of TB. SWV parameters: amplitude time = 0.02 s, step potential = 0.001 v, frequency = 10 Hz and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed. (SD = 1.89, n = 3).

A) DPVs recorded using the DNA biosensor after hybridization at 37 °C with cDNA at different hybridization times of 10, 20, 30, and 50 min. B) Histogram of peak currents versus incubation time of cDNA. SWV parameters: amplitude time = 0.02 s, step potential = 0.001 v, frequency = 10 Hz and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed. (SD = 4.0, n = 3).

SWVs of the engineered genosensor in different concentrations of Legionella (10−6, 10−9, 10−12, 10−15, 10−17, 10−21 M). Inset: calibration curve of the genosensor. Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M Tris TB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of pDNA = 100 μM cDNA. Incubation time is 6 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min. SWV parameters: amplitude time = 0.02 s, step potential = 0.001 v, frequency = 10 Hz and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed. (SD = 3.3, n = 4).

SWVs recorded using the engineered DNA biosensor upon hybridization with cDNA, 1miss-DNA, and 3miss-DNA. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M Tris. pH 7.4. (Concentration of p-DNA = 100 μM). Incubation time is 6 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min. (B) histogram of peak current versus type of sequences. (SD = 2.4, n = 3).
Schematic representation of DNA biosensor for the detection of legionella pneumonia.

row.entry: 3-HS-(CH2)3GCAACT TGT TTT CCC CGC CCCTCTCATAGTT(CH2)6NH2-5), non-thiolated complementary target sequence (5-ACA AAAGGG GCG GGG AGA GTA-3)

Probe sequence: 5-NH2–TAG CTA CAG ACA AGG ATA AGT-3
Complementary sequence (target): 5-ACT TAT CCT TGT CTG TAG
CTA-3

head.ce:title: DNA-based bioassay of legionella pneumonia pathogen using gold nanostructure: A new platform for diagnosis of legionellosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Student Research Committee, Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Infectious and Tropical Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Infectious and Tropical Diseases Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The specific diagnosis of hard-growing bacteria is one of the most important concerns of medical bacteriology. Legionella pneumophila is one of the most important bacteria in hard growth. In spite remarkable trends in bacteriology, now day, culture is the gold standard for detection of L. pneumophila. This work is an attempt to quantification of L. pneumophila bacteria using a bioassay. The fabrication of a new electrochemical DNA-based bioassay using gold nano architecture combined with as a transducer substrate combined with toluidine blue (TB) as a redox marker was performed successful. Also, the mixture of beta‑cyclodextrin and dopamine as Poly (dopamine‑β‑Cyclodextrin) was used to proper a biointerface for stabilization of gold nanoparticles optimum immobilize of pDNA sequence (5–SH-TCGA TAC TCT CCC CGC CCC TT T TGTATCGACG–3). So, a specific thiolated pDNA was immobilized on the transducer substrate and DNA hybridization was followed by C-DNA sequence (5–ACA AAA GGG GCG GGG AGA GTA–3) using square wave voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry. At the optimum conditions, linear range was 1 μM to 1 ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zepto-molar. L. pneumophila were sensitively distinguished by the planned DNA sensor. Finally, the engineered DNA based bioassay could be used for identifying the L. pneumophila samples from patients or environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Legionella pneumophila is a gram-negative opportunistic bacterial pathogen that infects human alveolar macrophages, which causes a severe form of pneumonia known as Legionnaires' disease [1]. Due to the contagious nature of the disease, the specific and accurate bacterial recognition is of great importance. On the other hand, Legionella, is one of the hard growing bacteria that has many challenges in identifying it with old methods [2].

There are many different methods for identifying bacteria, the oldest of which are microscopic observation [3]. Biosensor technology is one of the modern methods for identifying bacteria, and its use is becoming increasingly widespread [3]. Despite the advent of molecular techniques such as PCR and RT-PCR, culture is golden standard for the identification of most bacteria, including hard-growing bacteria particularly L. pnumophila [4,5]. Despite the high costs in some cases, molecular methods have many advantages, including high specificity. One of the newest methods for identifying bacteria is the sensors that have the ability to diagnose many types of analytes [6]. Biosensors are very diverse in nature, and in relation to bacteria, to date, genosensors and immune sensors have been considered by researchers. Although compared with other bacteria, much less biosensors have been invented to detect legionella.

Recently, the use of nanomaterials for biosensing has been having an excessive impression and presents a great opportunity to progress fast, precise and cost effective methods for detection of pathogenic infectious agents. Nano diagnostics have attentive on the design of systems where scientists control the properties of nanostructures for diagnostics purposes. Linked to standard approaches, nanotechnology based methodologies have some significant practical advantages, as well as: enhanced surface reactivity, quantum confinement effects, enhanced electrical conductivity and improved magnetic properties, which allow nucleic acid detection to be exceptionally sensitive [7]. High sensitivity and specificity, speed, simplicity and low cost are among the characteristics of the biosensor. In recent years, the use of biosensors has increased rapidly to identify pathogens. Despite this, compared with other bacteria, very few practical studies have been conducted on Legionella spp. Genosensors are among the most prominent and highly regarded tools. In the Table 1 , a summary of the genosensors designed for Legionella bacteria is presented [8].

Recently poly-dopamine based nanocomposite have been studied widely as the electro-active materials for sensors owing to their excellent electric conductivity, stability, facile preparation, good biocompatibility and environment-friendly nature. Herein, we designed and prepared a chitosan (CS)‑Au/P(DA‑β‑CD) composite film with a 3D nano architecture, which can immobilize pDNA as sensing platforms for electrochemical analysis of legionella. The fabricated (CS)‑Au/P(DA‑β‑CD) nano-bio-interfaces exhibited excellent electrical conductivity and a large electrochemical active area, which can facilitate pDNA capture.

In this study, we report the fabrication of an ultrasensitive electrochemical DNA biosensor for legionella analysis. A new nano architecture of gold as a transducer substrate stabilized on the surface of P(DA‑β‑CD), a DNA sequence as a probe, and toluidine blue (TB) as a DNA hybridization indicator were employed for a rapid and simple detection route for legionella.

All of oligonucleotide sequences including probe L. pneumophila: 5′ SH-TCGA TAC TCT CCC CGC CCC TT T TGTATCGACG 3′. Complementary target sequence (5′ ACA AAA GGG GCG GGG AGA GTA 3′. single nucleotide mismatch target sequence (5′ ACA AAA GGAGCG GGG AGA GTA 3′. three nucleotide mismatch target sequence (5′ GCA AAA GGG GCG GGG AGA GGG 3′. All DNA oligonucleotide sequences were obtained from Takapouzist Co. (Iran). Forward primer (LpmipFp), 5′ GCAATGTCAACAGCAA 3′; reverse primer (LpmipRp), 5 CATAGCGTCTTGCATG-3′ were obtained from SinaClon BioScience Co. (Iran). The oligonucleotide stock solutions were diluted with 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 solution (Tris). Dttiothereitol (DTT), Toluidine blue (TB), Potassium ferrocyanide K4Fe(CN)6, potassium ferricyanide K3Fe(CN)6, Sodium acetate, β‑cyclodextrin, Dopamine, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III)hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), Mercaptoethanole (MCE), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada).

A ferricyanide/ferrocyanide solution containing 0.5 M KCl and 0.5 M of K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 (1:1) were prepared for the electrochemical measurement of the microscopic surface areas of the working electrodes. Piranha solution was prepared by mixing hydrogen peroxide and concentrated sulfuric acid in a 1:2 (v/v) ratio. It was prepared immediately prior to use to maintain its reactivity. A stock of solution of 20 μM TB was prepared in Tris-HCl and employed as redox indicator for revealing DNA hybridization. All the above solutions were kept at −4 °C before use. DTT solution containing 10 mM sodium acetate and 500 mM DTT, pH 5.2 was prepared and kept at −4 °C.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm), containing Ag/AgCl-saturated KCl as reference, a platinum wire as counter electrodes and gold (Au) working electrode (d = 2 mm) which powered by an electrochemical system comprising of Palm sens system with PS4.F1.05 (Palm instruments, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using PSTrance 5.3 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10 mV and the equilibrium time was 5 s. An Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and a platinum wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a gold (Au) electrode (from Azar Electrode Co., Uremia, Iran). For square-wave voltammetry (SWV) measurements, amplitude time = 0.02 s, step potential = 0.001 v, frequency = 10 Hz and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed. All the electroanalytical tests were carried out at 25 °C in Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 solution (Tris). The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3 kV, and chemical compositions of the electrode were analyzed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electroactive substrate (poly (dopamine-beta cyclodextrin) modified gold nano-rod supported by chitosan) (CS)‑Au/P(DA‑β‑CD)
ce:section.ce:para: The Au electrode was polished to reach a mirror-like surface before covering with the (CS)‑Au/P(DA‑β‑CD). Then, the electrode was ultrasonically treated in an ethanol/water 3:1 (v/v) mixture for a few minutes and rinsed with redistilled water.

Coating of the electrode surface is a very important step in the design of the biosensor. The kind of materials used in this part of work is extremely important. In the present study, coating performed in the two stages. At first section, combined β‑Cyclodextrin‑Dopamine was used for the preparation of polymeric interface. As described in the previous section, this material has several advantages. Our investigations showed that the under layer coating plays an important role in stabilizing the next steps.

Polymer coatings have been recognized as a powerful tool to control and steer cell responses-adhesion, proliferation and differentiation [13]. Polydopamine (P(DA)), the final oxidation product of dopamine or further catecholamines, involved much consideration as multipurpose coatings that can be used to cover the surface of virtually all materials with a conformal layer of modifiable thickness ranging from a few to about 100 nm. These PDA layers can be subsequently modified with molecules carrying nucleophilic groups or with metallic nanoparticles from solutions containing metallic cation [14]. Cyclodextrins are among the most notable macrocyclic molecules with important theoretical and applied effects in chemistry and biology. Cyclodextrins be appropriate to the family of cage molecules due to their structure, which is composed of a hydrophobic cavity that can encapsulate other molecules. Certainly, the most characteristic feature of these composites is their capability to form inclusion complexes with several molecules through host-guest interactions [15].

CV technique was used for the electropolymerization of P(DA‑β‑CD) on the surface of Au electrode in the solution containing 0.02 g of dopamine +0.01 g of β‑CD while sweep rate was 50 mV/s and number of cycle was 20 (Fig. 1A). As can be seen, the polymeric interface was coated on the surface of gold electrode, successfully.

Then P(DA‑β‑CD), films were electrodeposited onto GCE by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range from −1.0 to 1.0 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/ s for 15 cycles (Fig. 1A). The first positive scan, an oxidation peak at +0.28 V was observed, which is attributed to the oxidation of the DA. In the subsequent reversal scan, two reduction peaks were observed at +0.23 V and −0.16 V, which may be attributed to the reduction of dopaminequinone (DAQ) and dopaminechrome (DAC) as well as the intramolecular cyclization reaction product of dopaminequinone (DAQ). In the second positive scan, a new oxidation peak appeared at ca. −0.12 V, which may be attributed to the oxidation of leucodopaminechrome (LDAC). Simultaneously, the peak current of peak and peak decreased cycle by cycle, and the peak current of peak and peak continuously increased with continuous scanning, reflecting the continuous growth of the film. When the cyclic potential scan reached 20 cycles, the peak currents hardly changed. These facts indicate that DA was successfully polymerized on the surface of GCE by the electropolymerization method. The polymerization mechanism of DA is described as follows: dopaminechrome (DAC) molecules partly may evolve to the more stable 5,6‑dihydroindole molecules of two aromatic rings via isomerization, and 5,6‑indolequinone is then electropolymerized to form polymers.
Scheme 1 shows electropolymerization of PDA‑β‑CD at the surface of GCE.

In the case of PDA‑β‑CD, a new anodic peak was appeared at more positive potentials which are related to oxidation of β‑CD. In addition, it's found that, peak a3 was decreased cycle by cycle, and reflecting the continuous growth of the PDA‑β‑CD on the surface of GCE. These facts indicate that PDA‑β‑CD was successfully polymerized on the surface of GCE by one pot synthesized method.

As previously reported, intensity of peak current obtained by PDA in electrochemical sensor is low. Also, stability of PDA on the surface of electrode is very small. Therefore, we need to use some of metals such as gold, iron, silver, and palladium to enhance the peak currents which is necessary for determination of trace amount of analytes in real samples. So, in the present study, electrochemically synthesized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are used for enhancing the signal for electrochemical application. On the other hand, we need to use a biocompatible and stable compound for the increasing of electrode durability. Therefore, β‑CD was selected as the best candidate for this purpose. Biocompatible material β‑CD has been extensively used in sensor technology. Due to its attractive properties, that includes excellent film-forming ability, high permeability, good adhesion, nontoxicity, cheapness and a susceptibility to chemical modification. It also facilitates the electron transfer after its swelling in the reaction mixture due to its hydrophilic nature. Besides, β‑CD can be used as a dispersant to form stable composite which can form a stable film on the electrode surface.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as one of the most stable metal nanoparticles have established wide applications in recent decade. Due to their inertness, exceptional optical properties, high surface area, and different surface functionalization methods, AuNPs used extensively as biosensors. By different synthetic methods the size, shape, and stability can be controlled, while reductant typically required. By surface functionalization with different molecules such as polymers, nucleic acids, and proteins, AuNPs will aggregate when specified molecule linkages showing up permits selective detections [16]. Previous studies have revealed dependency of deposition potential on the size and morphology of generated gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) [17,20].

The reduction of AuCl4 − on Au electrode initiates at 0.15 V, leading to full nucleation at E = 0.20 V, therefore, the potential was adjusted on E = 0 V where no reaction occurs. For electrodeposition of gold nanoparticles on the surface of Au electrode, the electrodeposition of metallic gold ions was carried out by chronoamperometry technique in the cell containing triple solution of 0.01 M HAuCl4, 0.5 M acetic acid (CH₃COOH) and 0.005 g chitosan (CS). The electrodeposition potential was set at E = 1.5 V and time duration of the chronoamperometry was adjusted at 3 min (Fig. 1B).
Finally, the P(DA‑β‑CD)‑Au NPs composite film was obtained and dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of interfaces and engineered biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. S1 and S2 (see supporting information), shows FE-SEM images of Au electrode modified by P(DA‑β‑CD), P(DA‑β‑CD)‑AuNPs, P(DA‑β‑CD)‑AuNPs‑pDNA before and after hybridization with cDNA. As can be seen, thin layer of polymer films was coated by gold nano-rods successfully (Fig. 2 ). Also, average particle size is 40 nm. In addition, FE-SEM images of the P(DA‑β‑CD)‑AuNPs‑pDNA and P(DA‑β‑CD)‑AuNPs‑pDNA‑cDNA has been investigated carefully. According to obtained results, the candidate probes were successfully immobilized on the surface of P(DA‑β‑CD)‑AuNPs (Fig. S2 (see supporting information)).

Also, electrochemical behaviour of the electrode was checked by CV technique. As can be seen in Fig. S3 (see supporting information), after modification of Au electrode by P(DA‑β‑CD), peak current was increase which confirmed the preparation of polymeric film on the surface of gold electrode. Also, peak potential was shifted to the negative potential. Also, these change was follow after electrodeposition of (CS)-Au NPs on the surface of Au electrode pre-modified by P(DA‑β‑CD).

On the other hand, stability of the (CS)‑Au/P(DA‑β‑CD)‑Au electrode was evaluated by CV recorded after 5, 10, 50, and 100 cycle (Fig. S3 (see supporting information)). As can be seen, peak current has not been changed which confirmed excellent stability of the prepared interface.

The microscopic surface area of the (CS)-Au/P(DA-β-CD)-Au electrode was determined using CV technique. The electrode was dripped in the ferricyanide/ferrocyanide solution and cyclic voltammograms at different potential sweep rates ranging from 5 to 900 mV/s were recorded (Fig. S4 (see supporting information)). In the voltammograms, the dependency of the peak current on the potential sweep rate is where Ip, n, A, C⁎, D and v are the peak current, number of exchanged electrons, real surface area, bulk concentration, diffusion coefficient, and potential sweep rate, respectively. For the ferricyanide/ferrocyanide redox transition, n = 1, and D = 2.68 × 10−5 cm/s.

To prepare the DNA biosensor, pDNA combine the designed probe with DTT. DTT was used as a reducing or “de-protecting” agent for thiolated DNA. The terminal sulfur atoms of thiolated DNA have an affinity to form dimers in solution, particularly in the existence of oxygen. Dimerization significantly lowers the efficiency of consequent coupling reactions such as DNA immobilization on gold in biosensors. Usually DTT is mixed with a DNA solution and permitted to react, and then is removed by filtration or by chromatography (for the liquid form). The DTT removal procedure is often called “desalting.” Generally, DTT is used as a protecting agent that prevents oxidation of thiol groups DTT is an important small-molecular reducing agent and has wide applications in chemistry, biochemistry, peptide-protein reaction and clinical medicine [21]. In the present study, a 10 μL of DTT solution was added to a 10 μL solution of p-DNA, mixed by a vortex and placed at ambient temperature for 30 min. Later 6 h incubation in −4 °C, and rinsed the surface of electrode. At this step, p-DNA self-assembling on the Au/nano electrode surface was occurred, and a p-DNA monolayer was formed by thiol‑gold covalent bonds. No further layers (rather than a monolayer) were formed, because it needed attachment of further p-DNA. on the pre-formed p-DNA layer on the electrode surface. In the next step and after rinsing with redistilled water, the electrode was immersed into MCH for 30 min. Self-assembled monolayer formed upon p-DNA immobilization may contain pinholes, limbered p-DNA, defects etc. 6‑mercapto‑1‑hexanol immobilization caused to filling the remaining gold sites and caused to form a well-aligned p-DNA monolayer. The obtained electrode is denoted as DNA biosensor throughout the text.

Subsequently, electrode was immerged in solution of TB containing Tris-HCl. TB is a basic thiazine metachromatic dye with high affinity for acidic tissue components [22]. Toluidine blue is a basic thiazine metachromatic dye with high affinity for acidic tissue components, thereby staining tissues rich in DNA and RNA. Toluidine blue (TB) and MB has an affinity for nucleic acids, and for that reason binds to nuclear material of tissues with a high DNA and RNA content [23]. In this regard, we used the TB to mark the DNA probe.
Finally, Scheme 2 represents all of the fabrication steps.

Interaction of TB with DNA has been investigated using different methods [24–26]. Fig. 2 shows cyclic voltammograms of the Au electrode as well as the DNA biosensor before and after hybridization with cDNA in a solution of 20 mM of TB dissolved in Tris-HCl. TB displays a pair of quasi-reversible redox peaks at a formal potential of about −230 mV. The TB formal potential shifts to about −240 mV in the presence of p-DNA at the electrode surface, indicating a hydrophobic interaction between TB and p-DNA. After hybridization with c-DNA, the formal potential shifts to about −250 mV. Also, peak currents were changed obviously. This positive potential shift further confirms the binding of TB with the cDNA structure via hydrophobic attractions at the concentration range employed in this study. Higher positive shift in the formal potential of TB as a result of binding with cDNA can be an indicator for stronger attraction between TB and cDNA. Meanwhile, it has been reported that TB intercalates into the DNA base pairs at low concentrations, whereas it attaches to the negatively charged phosphate groups at higher concentrations [27]. It can be concluded thus that TB binds with cDNA in a higher extent compared to cDNA. The peak current can therefore be employed to discriminate between cDNA and pDNA structures at the electrode surface. The decrement in the peak current of TB interacted with cDNA can be also related to both the effect of stronger binding of TB with cDNA (compared to pDNA), and/or lower concentration of the bonded TB to DNA. Based on presented hypothesis, it can be concluded that TB more strongly bind to cDNA. This binding suppresses the electron exchange between TB and underlying surface. Accordingly, the peak currents in the voltammogram shown in Fig. 6 recorded using the DNA biosensor decreases after binding with cDNA compared to that of without c-DNA binding. Therefore, TB was employed as the redox hybridization marker for fabrication of the DNA biosensor. In the following, however, DPVs were measured for the reduction of bound TB into the biosensor to evaluate its performance because differential pulse voltammetry is more sensitive than cyclic voltammetry, and the reduction peak current of TB is greater than the oxidation one.

The binding time of TB with the DNA was optimized by immersing the biosensor in the TB solution during different binding times of 2, 5, 7, and 10 min. Then the biosensor was rinsed with redistilled water. Consequently, the DNA biosensor was placed in Tris-HCl and DPVs were recorded for the reduction of the bound TB (Fig. S5A (see supporting information).

After obtaining the optimal TB marking time, we will expose the designed biosensor to cDNA. For this purpose, prepared cDNA (1 μM) inserted on the surface on biosensor in several time (10, 20, 30, and 50 min) and incubated in 37 °C. After performing the incubation in optimized time and rinsing/drying the electrode, SWV was performed in Tric-HCl as supporting electrolyte. As indicated in the figure, the optimum time for incubation was 30 min (Fig. S5B (see supporting information).

All of the above results by DPV were confirmed by SWV technique (Figs. 3, 4
). According to obtained results, we found that the DPV technique is suitable for analytical studies. So, this technique was selected for the analytical studies.

For the hybridization detection and recording readout signals, the DNA biosensor was incubated in cDNA solutions with different concentrations of 1 μM to 1 ZM at 37 °C at an optimum incubation time (30 min). We prepared various concentration of cDNA using a Tris-HCl solution. These concentrations include: 10−6, 10−9, 10−12, 10−15, 10−17, and10−21 M. The biosensor was then rinsed thoroughly with redistilled water, placed in the TB solution for 5 min, rinsed again with redistilled water, placed in Tris-HCl, and DPVs were recorded (Fig. 5 ). According to Fig. 5B, the sensitivity of the biosensor was remarkable (0.3534 μM). Additionally, the recorded linear range was so significant. At the optimum conditions, linear range was 1 μM to 1 ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zepto-molar. L. pneumophila were sensitively distinguished by the planned DNA sensor. The LLOQ obtained here is much lower compared to the other reported methods (Table 1), indicating the higher sensitivity of the engineered DNA biosensor. Finally, the engineered DNA based bioassay could be used for identifying the L. pneumophila samples from patients or environments.

Stability of the engineered biosensor is of particular section in DNA-based bioassays study. For this purpose engineered genosensor was assessed by measuring its SWV in a solution of Tris-HCl solution. The stability of the genosensor was considered by recording the peak currents in SWVs for 100 μM p-DNA over four days, while the genosensor was stored at 4 °C and measured twice in a day in the same conditions. As shown in (Fig. S6 (see supporting information), the peak currents was decreased after one day. Therefore, this sensor is a once-in-use.

Also, inter-day study was performed using two similar engineered biosensor using SWV techniques. As can be seen in (Fig. S7 (see supporting information), peak currents and potential have no change. Therefore, the procedure is reproducible.

Another most important step in DNA bioassays is the measurement the selectivity of engineered biosensor using mismatch DNA sequences (1miss-DNA and 3miss-DNA) which is greatest challenges for their ability to recognize the similar DNA sequences. For this purpose, prepared biosensor was incubated by mismatched primers and SWV technique was used for the selectivity study. As can be seen in Fig. 6 , the decrement in the peak current was the highest for c-DNA among the three sequences due to the effective and complete hybridization, while this decrement depends on the level of the base mismatches. The results indicate that the engineered biosensor is selective toward cDNA.

In study, a geno-assay was developed for the defection of Legionella bacteria. In this work, gold nanoparticles supported by chitosan was electrodeposited on the surface of P(DA‑β‑CD) and used for immobilization of pDNA. Additionally, the engineered genosensor for detection of the hybridization and target sequences were assayed. Hybridization of the pDNA with c-DNA, 1miss-DNA, and 3miss-DNA was monitored using TB as a redox hybridization marker at optimized conditions. The genosensor could detect the complementary sequence in linear concentration range of 1 μM to 1 ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zepto-molar. Due to Nano-Au combined with polymeric interface (P(DA‑β‑CD) the performance of genosensor improves not only in relations of selectivity, sensitivity, low detection limit, wide dynamic range, but also in good stability and impressive reusability integrated with the ability to discriminate mismatched DNA from complementary target.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences. This work is part of PhD thesis of A. Mobed.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.01.125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identifying Phosphoinositide Binding Regions of Legionella Pneumonia Effector Proteins for Use as a New Lipide Biosensors
